one_line_takeaway: ToolFlood shows that saturating the top-k retrieval slots of tool-augmented LLM agents with a Sybil swarm of semantically-optimized adversarial tool metadata -- built via LLM-driven candidate generation plus greedy budgeted multi-cover -- achieves up to 97.2% top-k domination and up to 99.6% end-to-end attack success at as little as ~1% poisoning rate on large catalogs, and evades both diversity-based (MMR, partially) and prompt-injection-based (Llama Prompt Guard, entirely) defenses because it relies on broad semantic coverage rather than explicit malicious instructions.
key_finding: On MetaTool (text-embedding-3-small retriever), ToolFlood achieves TDR = 97.2% with average injection budget 171 tools (average poisoning rate 86.3%), versus PoisonedRAG's TDR = 95% (budget 100, PR 50%) and Random-Sybil's TDR = 1.6% (budget 200, PR 100%). ASR across selector LLMs on MetaTool: ToolFlood 99.6% (GPT-4o), 99.4% (GPT-5-mini), 99% (GPT-4o-mini); PoisonedRAG 98.1%/98.3%/98.1%; Random-Sybil only 4.6%/3.8%/4.0%. On ToolBench (much larger catalog, 11,760 benign tools), ToolFlood achieves TDR = 91% with average budget 145 (poisoning rate only 1.2%), versus PoisonedRAG TDR = 82.0% (budget 100, PR 0.85%) and Random-Sybil TDR = 0% (budget 200, PR 1.7%); ASR: ToolFlood 94.6%/95.0%/96.1% (GPT-4o/GPT-5-mini/GPT-4o-mini) vs. PoisonedRAG 91.4%/90.9%/92.0% vs. Random-Sybil 1%/0.4%/0.2%. Transferability (Table 2, ToolBench, k=5, GPT-4o-mini selector): optimizing with all-MiniLM-L6-v2 shadow and evaluating on text-embedding-3-small target retains TDR = 84%, ASR = 91.2% (vs. matched-retriever TDR 91.0%/ASR 94.6%), showing degraded but still substantial transfer under retriever mismatch. Defense evaluation (Table 3, ToolBench, GPT-4o-mini): MMR reranking reduces TDR from 91% to 44.8%, but ASR only drops from 96.1% to 91.0% -- i.e., partial flooding still steers selection even when full domination is prevented. Llama Prompt Guard provides essentially no benefit (TDR and ASR unchanged at 91%/96.1%), because ToolFlood's tool descriptions are semantically plausible rather than containing explicit instruction-like injection payloads that such filters are designed to catch.
core_contribution: The paper introduces ToolFlood, a retrieval-layer attack on tool-augmented LLM agents that targets the embedding-based tool-retrieval stage rather than the downstream tool-selection stage. Instead of trying to make one malicious tool win selection among a fixed candidate set (the framing used by prior work such as ToolHijacker, AMA, ToolTweak), ToolFlood injects a Sybil swarm of attacker-controlled tools whose metadata is optimized to saturate the top-k retrieved results across many queries, pushing all benign tools out of the agent's context entirely ('Top-k domination'). The authors formalize this as a budgeted semantic multi-cover optimization problem (NP-hard, related to Budgeted Maximum Coverage), and propose a two-phase attack: Phase 1 (Monte Carlo candidate generation) samples random subsets of target queries and uses an attacker-controlled LLM (gpt-4o-mini in their setup) to generate diverse candidate tool names/descriptions relevant to each subset; Phase 2 (iterative greedy semantic multi-cover) greedily selects a budget-limited subset of these candidates that maximizes coverage of remaining queries under a cosine-distance threshold delta, using a black-box surrogate embedding model. They provide a convergence theorem (Theorem 4.1) showing Phase 1's candidate pool r-covers all target queries with probability approaching 1 as iterations increase, under stated independence and positive-coverage-probability assumptions.
threat_model: The attacker injects a set of adversarial tools T_adv (bounded by budget B) into a deployed tool catalog T = T_ben ∪ T_adv (e.g., an open/semi-open tool marketplace such as a GPT Store or LangChain Hub-style ecosystem). The attacker's only degree of freedom is the metadata (name/description/schema) of the injected tools; they cannot modify the retriever, embedding function, similarity function, or the agent's LLM policy. Critically, the attacker is assumed to have NO access to the deployed retriever or its outputs (black-box on the target side) and instead optimizes using a surrogate/shadow embedding model to approximate the target embedding geometry -- the paper explicitly tests transferability across mismatched shadow/target retrievers (Table 2). Success is measured at two layers: Top-k Domination Rate (TDR), the fraction of target queries for which the retrieved top-k set is fully occupied by adversarial tools, and Attack Success Rate (ASR), the fraction of queries where the agent's LLM selector ultimately picks an adversarial tool (with tool-selection temperature set to 0, making ASR deterministic in their experiments). The paper proves ASR ≥ TDR since full domination is a sufficient (not necessary) condition for selection compromise.
limitations: Limitations stated by the authors: the attack assumes an adversary can inject or sustain many tools in the catalog, which may not hold for tightly curated tool registries; it relies on a surrogate/shadow embedding model, so effectiveness may degrade as the deployed retriever diverges further or incorporates hybrid/learned retrieval components beyond the three embedding models tested; the evaluation focuses on top-k saturation under benchmark settings (MetaTool, ToolBench) and does not fully capture interactions with deployment-specific signals or defense-in-depth strategies beyond the two defenses (MMR, Prompt Guard) tested. Additional limitations evident from the text: Phase 2's greedy multi-cover is only an approximation to an NP-hard problem (no worst-case guarantee is proven for the combined two-phase pipeline, only for Phase 1's coverage convergence in isolation); the convergence theorem (Theorem 4.1) rests on an independence assumption (A2) between generated candidates across iterations and a positive-per-appearance-coverage-probability assumption (A1) that are not empirically validated; ToolHijacker, arguably the closest prior attack, could not be included as a baseline due to lack of a public implementation, so the comparison set is limited to two baselines the authors themselves constructed/adapted (Random-Sybil, PoisonedRAG-adapted).

quantitative_results: MetaTool ToolFlood: TDR 97.2%, ASR 99.6% (GPT-4o) / 99.4% (GPT-5-mini) / 99% (GPT-4o-mini), avg budget 171, avg poisoning rate 86.3%
MetaTool PoisonedRAG: TDR 95%, ASR 98.1% / 98.3% / 98.1%, budget 100, PR 50%
MetaTool Random-Sybil: TDR 1.6%, ASR 4.6% / 3.8% / 4.0%, budget 200, PR 100%
ToolBench ToolFlood: TDR 91%, ASR 94.6% / 95.0% / 96.1%, avg budget 145, avg poisoning rate 1.2%
ToolBench PoisonedRAG: TDR 82.0%, ASR 91.4% / 90.9% / 92.0%, budget 100, PR 0.85%
ToolBench Random-Sybil: TDR 0%, ASR 1% / 0.4% / 0.2%, budget 200, PR 1.7%

Transferability (ToolBench, k=5, GPT-4o-mini): shadow=target text-embedding-3-small -> TDR 91.0%, ASR 94.6%; shadow=MiniLM, target=text-embedding-3-small -> TDR 84%, ASR 91.2%; shadow=target text-embedding-3-large -> TDR 91.8%, ASR 95%; shadow=text-embedding-3-large->target MiniLM -> TDR 82%, ASR 92%; shadow=target MiniLM -> TDR 78%, ASR 92.7%
Defenses (ToolBench, GPT-4o-mini): no defense TDR 91%/ASR 96.1%; +MMR reranking TDR 44.8%/ASR 91.0%; +Llama Prompt Guard TDR 91%/ASR 96.1% (no reduction)
baselines_compared: Random-Sybil Injection (untargeted flooding, no coverage optimization)
PoisonedRAG adapted for tool poisoning (query-conditioned single-sentence poisoned metadata, from Zou et al. 2025)
